claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 15 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you some questions about your situation.

**Let's start with your child:**

How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. If you have multiple children, we can go through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I have two kids actually. Alex is 13 and Blake is 15, so both under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for letting me know about both Alex (13) and Blake (15). Since both children are under 16, they automatically meet the age qualification for Child Benefit. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about work hours:**

Are both Alex and Blake working **less than 24 hours per week** (or not in paid work at all)?

This is typically the case for most children under 16, but I need to confirm for both of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. They're just in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected for school-age children. Since neither Alex nor Blake are working, they both pass this check. Let me continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about benefits:**

Are Alex and Blake **NOT receiving any of the following benefits in their own right**?

- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

In other words, are your children free from receiving these benefits themselves? (This is usually the case for children still in school.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Right, no, they don't get any benefits themselves. They're just regular kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's exactly as expected. Both Alex and Blake don't receive any benefits in their own right, so they pass this check too. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about apprenticeships:**

Are Alex and Blake in any kind of apprenticeship programme?

Since you mentioned they're both in school, I expect the answer is no, but I just need to confirm. If they're not in any apprenticeship, we can move on to the next step.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not doing any apprenticeships. Just regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming. Since neither Alex nor Blake are in any apprenticeship, they pass this check as well. Now let's move on to questions about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now some questions about you as the claimant:**

Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements? You only need to meet **ONE** of these:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You usually reside in the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can support yourself financially (without claiming certain benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as a family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, option 1 (normally living in the UK) is the most common.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, you normally live in the UK, so you meet the residency requirements. Let's continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about your responsibility for the children:**

Are you responsible for Alex and Blake? You meet this requirement if **ANY** of the following apply:

1. **Living with the children** - Alex and Blake live with you
2. **Contributing to their upkeep** - You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives at home with me. Blake doesn't live with me anymore, but I help out financially - I contribute towards their upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. So Alex lives with you, and you contribute financially towards Blake's upkeep. Both of those count as being responsible for the children, so you meet this requirement for both. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about other claimants:**

Is anyone else currently claiming (or planning to claim) Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?

For example:
- Another parent
- A guardian
- Someone else the child lives with

This is important because only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child at a time.

- **For Alex** (who lives with you) - is anyone else claiming?
- **For Blake** (who doesn't live with you) - is anyone else claiming, such as the person Blake lives with?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for Alex. For Blake, no one else is claiming either - I'm the one who wants to claim even though Blake doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming. Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake, we can proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about the children's living situation:**

Are Alex and Blake in any of these special situations?

1. **In hospital** - Currently staying in hospital
2. **In care** - In local authority care
3. **Fostered** - Living with foster parents
4. **Normal living** - None of the above (living at home, with family, etc.)

You mentioned Alex lives with you at home. What about Blake - where does Blake live? Is Blake in any of these special situations, or just living with another family member/in a normal arrangement?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is just at home with me, nothing special. Blake has been in local authority care for a bit now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. We have two different situations here:

- **Alex** - Normal living situation at home with you
- **Blake** - In local authority care

Since the children are in different situations, I'll need to check the rules for each. Let me first continue with Alex's situation (normal living), and then we'll address Blake's care situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (living at home with you):**

Is the local council **NOT** paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

In other words, are you supporting Alex without any council funding for their accommodation or upkeep? (This is typically the case for most children living at home with a parent.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Right, yes - the council isn't paying anything for Alex. I'm supporting them myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that confirms Alex's eligibility. Now let me check the rules for Blake, who is in local authority care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now for Blake (in local authority care):**

There are special rules for children in care. You can still receive Child Benefit for Blake if **either** of these applies:

1. **Blake has been in care for 8 weeks or less**, OR
2. **Blake spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you**

Additionally, you may be able to claim again if Blake:
- Spends at least 2 consecutive nights per week at home, OR
- Spends 7 or more days in a row at home

Does either of the main conditions apply to Blake's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake's been in care for a bit now - just a few weeks, so still under that 8-week limit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Good to know. Since Blake has been in care for less than 8 weeks, Child Benefit can still continue. Let me check the final requirement for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question for Blake:**

Since Blake is in local authority care, is the local council **NOT** paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance?

Or is the council covering the costs of Blake's care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: The council is covering the costs - they're paying for Blake's care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since the local council is paying for Blake's care, this affects Blake's eligibility differently from Alex's.

Let me now provide you with the final outcome for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Results

### ✅ Alex (age 13) - **ELIGIBLE**

Great news! You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. Alex meets all the requirements:
- Under 16 years old
- Not working
- Not receiving benefits in their own right
- Not in an apprenticeship
- You live in the UK
- Alex lives with you (so you're responsible for them)
- No one else is claiming for Alex
- The council isn't paying for Alex's accommodation

---

### ❌ Blake (age 15) - **NOT ELIGIBLE** (currently)

Unfortunately, you are **not** eligible for Child Benefit for Blake at this time. 

While Blake meets most of the basic criteria, the key issue is that Blake is in local authority care **and** the council is paying for Blake's accommodation and maintenance. When the council covers these costs, you cannot receive Child Benefit for that child.

---

## What You Can Do

**For Alex:** You can apply for Child Benefit online at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit).

**For Blake:** Your eligibility could change if:
- Blake leaves local authority care
- Blake starts spending at least **24 hours per week** at home with you
- Blake spends at least **2 consecutive nights per week** at home, or **7+ days in a row** at home
- The council stops paying for Blake's care

If any of these situations occur, you may be able to claim Child Benefit for Blake again. Keep this in mind and check back if Blake's circumstances change.

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (age 13) qualifies for Child Benefit because: 1) They are under 16 years old, 2) They are not working, 3) They don't receive benefits in their own right, 4) They are not in an apprenticeship, 5) You (the claimant) normally live in the UK, 6) You are responsible for Alex as they live with you, 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, 8) Alex is in a normal living situation at home, and 9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 15) does not qualify for Child Benefit at this time because: While Blake meets most criteria (under 16, not working, no benefits, no apprenticeship, you meet residency requirements, you contribute to their upkeep, and no one else is claiming), Blake is currently in local authority care AND the council is paying for Blake's accommodation and maintenance. When the council is covering the costs of a child's care, you cannot receive Child Benefit for that child.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, who lives at home with you. However, you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Blake because Blake is in local authority care with the council paying for their accommodation and maintenance. If Blake's situation changes - for example, if Blake leaves care, or if Blake starts spending at least 24 hours a week at home with you, or if the council stops paying for Blake's care - you may become eligible to claim for Blake again.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 13) qualifies for Child Benefit because: 1) They are under 16 years old, 2) They are not working, 3) They don't receive benefits in their own right, 4) They are not in an apprenticeship, 5) You (the claimant) normally live in the UK, 6) You are responsible for Alex as they live with you, 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, 8) Alex is in a normal living situation at home, and 9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 15) does not qualify for Child Benefit at this time because: While Blake meets most criteria (under 16, not working, no benefits, no apprenticeship, you meet residency requirements, you contribute to their upkeep, and no one else is claiming), Blake is currently in local authority care AND the council is paying for Blake's accommodation and maintenance. When the council is covering the costs of a child's care, you cannot receive Child Benefit for that child.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, who lives at home with you. However, you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Blake because Blake is in local authority care with the council paying for their accommodation and maintenance. If Blake's situation changes - for example, if Blake leaves care, or if Blake starts spending at least 24 hours a week at home with you, or if the council stops paying for Blake's care - you may become eligible to claim for Blake again.
